challenges.title: Source Order Override
challenges.description: Write two CSS rules that both target `p` elements. The first rule should set `color` to `red`. The second rule should set `color` to `blue`. Because of source order in the cascade, the second rule wins. Assign your CSS to the variable `css`.
challenges.test_code:
assert css.lower().count('p') >= 2, 'Should have two rules targeting p'
assert 'color' in css.lower(), 'Should set the color property'
assert 'red' in css.lower(), 'Should use red in one rule'
assert 'blue' in css.lower(), 'Should use blue in another rule'
assert css.lower().index('red') < css.lower().index('blue'), 'Red rule should come before blue rule so blue wins by source order'
hints: When two rules have the same specificity, the one that appears last in the source wins.
Write two separate `p { color: ...; }` rules, one after the other.

challenges.title: Specificity and !important
challenges.description: Write three CSS rules that all set `color` on the same element: (1) a `p` element selector setting color to `red`, (2) a `.text` class selector setting color to `green`, (3) a `#intro` ID selector setting color to `blue`. Then add a fourth rule using the `p` element selector with `!important` to set color to `purple`. Assign your CSS to the variable `css`.
challenges.test_code:
assert 'p' in css.lower(), 'Should have a p element selector'
assert '.text' in css, 'Should have a .text class selector'
assert '#intro' in css, 'Should have an #intro ID selector'
assert 'red' in css.lower(), 'Should set color to red in element rule'
assert 'green' in css.lower(), 'Should set color to green in class rule'
assert 'blue' in css.lower(), 'Should set color to blue in ID rule'
assert '!important' in css, 'Should use !important'
assert 'purple' in css.lower(), 'Should set color to purple with !important'
hints: Specificity order (low to high): element < class < ID. But !important overrides all.
The !important syntax goes before the semicolon: `color: purple !important;`
